course_description:
This course explores key issues and debates in understanding Australia as a nation, a culture and a society. Topics examine the forces shaping contemporary Australia and their historical context. The course is designed for both local and international students. AUST1000 is an interdisciplinary course, just as Australian Studies is an interdisciplinary field. Students will be introduced to recent developments in the fields of media/cultural studies, literature, history, politics, Indigenous studies and gender studies. A range of cultural texts including film, television, print media and advertisements will be used to develop the key concepts of the course.

Employability Statement

This course develops a range of skills valued by employers. You will develop knowledge, attributes, skills, and experience in the following:

What makes Australia distinctive as a nation, a culture, and a society? What does it mean to be Australian? Where do ideas about Australianness come from and how are they reinforced? What role does media play in creating and maintaining concepts of Australian identity?

This course is intended as a general introduction to Australia and to Australian Studies for both local and international students. It is responsive to current debates, exploring the forces shaping contemporary Australia and analysing their historical formation. AUST1000 is particularly concerned with examining the social and cultural issues that have been associated with the ways that images or interpretations of Australia and Australian identity are defined and debated. It will give students the chance to explore topics through a range of mediums such as art, music, screen media, podcasts, journalism, and more.

This course challenges dominant discourses and ideologies. It has a strong focus on First Nations perspectives and the history of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people. Further, the course emphasises counternarratives and the standpoints of multicultural, refugee, asylum seeker, and LGBTQIA+ people.

The course code

Teaching staff do not have access to change tutorials or help with timetables; all timetabling changes must be processed through the Timetabling Team.
course_aims:
The course aims to develop an understanding of contemporary Australia,ﾠits people and its history through a range of themes, which are explored across the semester.ﾠﾠ

Key questions include:

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge of the central issues affecting contemporary understandings of Australia as a nation
Be able to compare, contrast, and analyse a range of different perspectives on current debates about Australian society and culture
Be able to make links between individual identities and national identities
Show reasonable knowledge of the historical context of current issues and recognise ways in which history is re-interpreted to meet contemporary needs
Demonstrate an understanding of concepts and methods important to the field of Australian Studies

assessment_details.title: Workshop Assessment Activities
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment takes place over a total of 10 workshops starting in Week 2 (2/03/2026) There will be 10 workshop assessment activities in total each worth 3%. Each workshop will consist of a selection of activities designed to expand and assess your understanding of the weekly topics. Workshop activities will further provide you with the opportunity to develop your skills in critical thinking, language development, communication, written literacy, observation and interpretation of media texts, and visual literacy. You must actively take part in all workshop activities to recieve a maximum mark of 3%. Specific details of each workshop, the activities you will be completing, and how to prepare for the workshop will be provided in advance on Blackboard. You will be required to read the instructions carefully and prepare for workshops before attending. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
To be completed in person in workshops only.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
This assessment task is to be completed in person. Students are NOT able to apply for an extension via the Unitask portal for this assessment item. Please contact your tutor/course coordinator directly to see if alternative arrangements are available.

assessment_details.title: Focused Analysis 1
assessment_details.task_description: For this assessment you will be required to choose an academic reading from the list provided on Blackboard . You will be required to respond to a set of questions about the reading designed to develop your ability to develop skills in critical thinking and the ability to question, analyse, interpret, evaluate, and synthesise information. Further instructions, including specific questions, assessment guidelines, grading criteria, and sample assessment will be available on Blackboard. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
TurnItIn Receipts:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
View the name of the submitted file View date and time of the upload Resubmit your paper (if necessary) Download your submitted paper Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Digital Exhibition Concept and Rationale
assessment_details.task_description: You have been asked to curate an exhibition that provides a multidimensional narrative about contemporary Australia. Your exhibition should not reproduce tired cliches and stereotypes about Australian identity, nor will your exhibition necessarily be a 'positive' or celebratory representation of the nation. Instead, it should seek to present Australia in new ways. The idea is to demonstrate what you have learned from the course by applying it creatively and meaningfully to media objects around us. Your exhibition will consist of five items and must address five DIFFERENT topics from Weeks 3 –11 of the course. A different form of media must be used for each exhibition piece (i.e. 1 poster, 1 gallery art piece, 1 magazine cover, 1 street mural, 1 event notice). Items must NOT have appeared in the course material or workshops. Items must be photographed by you and/or sourced using the research methods you have learned in the course (i.e. kept at the Fryer Library or the John Oxley Library, found on Trove, part of the AustLit database collection etc). Some examples of media that you could use include posters, cartoons, poetry, books, graffiti, street art/murals, statues, event notices, locations, gallery art, magazines, screen media (a film poster for example), social media projects. The choice is yours. The media piece itself does not have to be contemporary, but you do need to establish how it has shaped contemporary Australia (for example, you could use a historic artifact to show how it has shaped ways of thinking today). Each item will be accompanied by a 100-word (+/- 10%) explanation/caption that actively engages with a minimum of 1 academic, peer-reviewed resource from the course (i.e. a minimum of 1 academic source per explanation/caption). In addition, you will include a 1500-word (+/- 10%) rationale that contextualises your choice of exhibition items and what understandings of nation/national identify are conveyed by your items. You must support all claims in your rationale with evidence and include a full reference list (not included in the word count). Full guidelines, marking criteria, and examples will be provided on Blackboard. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: Introduction This week we will go through important information related to the course including Blackboard, assessments, learning approaches and what to expect. We will also begin looking at key critical issues and debates that have shaped, and continue to influence Australia today.

Week 2: Visual Thinking Strategies This week we will begin with a Visual Thinking Strategy Session, presented by the UQ Art Museum. This session will be vital to the development of critical thinking skills needed for this course as well your learning journey more broadly. VTS teaches learners to carefully observe, evaluate, synthesise, justify and speculate – all essential aspects for critical thinking. It has been measured as an effective tool to build visual thinking skills, which directly benefits graduate performance, including exam results. Learning outcomes: L05

Week 3: Australian National Identity: Mythology, Power, and Privilege This week we will critically examine some prominent myths about Australian national identity such as the myth of Terra Nullius, the myth of Egalitarianism and ‘a fair go’, and the ANZAC myth. We will ask questions such as: Whose interests do these mythologies serve? Who is included and who is rendered invisible? What do these mythologies tell us about Australia politically, socially, and culturally?

Week 4: Indigenous Identities: Activism and Resistance This week we will explore historical events that have shaped First Nations Peoples. We will look at government policies that have, and continue to, impacted First Nations peoples. We will also explore acts of resistance and activism by First Nation people from Australia’s first wars, the establishment of the Aboriginal Tent Embassy in Canberra, Nicky Winmar’s stand against racial discrimination, and more.

Week 5: Multicultural Identities in Australia The first migrants to Colonial Australia were prisoners of the British Crown who were transported to the country as punishment. Since then, there have been different waves of immigration that have established Australia as a multicultural nation - an identity that has been subject to both celebration and controversy. This week we will track historical attitudes, policies, and practices that have informed cultural ideas about immigration and national identity.

Week 6: Gender and sexuality in Contemporary Australia This week we will look at hegemony and how tropes and ideologies about gender operate to maintain hegemonic power structures. However, our main focus will be to explore diverse, historically overlooked gender identities in Australian society.
Week 7: Fryer Library Excursion This week we will be going to the Fryer Library. This excursion will be valuable for your Digital Exhibition Concept and Rationale.

Week 8: Refugees and Asylum Seekers: Policies and Practice As we saw in Week 5, Colonial Australia has history of discrimination when it comes to the country’s borders and who it will allow in, dating back to the implementation of the Immigration Restriction Act , in 1901. Since the mid-1980s Australian governments have increasingly implemented hard lined immigration policies that has led the UN to warn that Australia's mandatory detention regime for refugees and asylum seekers is violating international human rights. This week we will examine some of the issues facing refugees and asylum seekers.

Week 9: Autographic Practice: Agency, Advocacy, and Representation This week we will look at the way asylum seekers, First Nations, and culturally/gender diverse people use autobiographical comics to voice issues of subjectivity, embodiment, and belonging. This week’s topic is designed to inspire you and get you thinking creatively about your digital exhibition concept and rationale.
PUBLIC HOLIDAY: NO SEMINARS THIS WEEK

Week 11: Contemporary Australia and Counternarratives on Social Media This week we will continue our exploration of alternative modes of contemporary storytelling by looking at the way Australian film makers and artists are using Social Media platforms to share alternative, culturally diverse narratives.

Week 12: Contemporary Australia in Focus: Truth Telling and Treaty, Why it Matters With the first treaty between Indigenous people and the Australian government recently passed in Victoria law recently, this is an opportune time to discuss why treaty is significant, what truth telling is, and why it is essential to reconciliation. We will also spend some time discussing your Digital Exhibition Concept and Rationale. Learning outcomes: L05
Week 13: Independent Study Week There will be no seminars this week. Instead it will be a time for you to finalise your Digital Exhibition Concept and Rationale.

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
